title: What Is Section 106 of the NHPA? A Complete Guide
content:
Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act is one of the most consequential federal environmental review requirements for any project involving federal permits, licenses, funding, or land. Whether you are a developer, a state agency, or a federal official, understanding Section 106 compliance is essential to avoiding costly project delays and legal challenges.

Quick Answer: Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act (NHPA) requires federal agencies to consider the effects of their undertakings on historic properties before approving permits or funding. The process involves identifying historic properties, assessing adverse effects, and consulting with stakeholders to resolve those effects.

Section 106 applies any time a federal nexus exists — meaning a federal permit, federal funding, or federal land is involved. The Advisory Council on Historic Preservation (ACHP) oversees the process, and state historic preservation offices (SHPOs) play a central role in consultation. For project sponsors unfamiliar with the regulatory framework, partnering with a qualified cultural resource management (CRM) firm is the most reliable way to navigate compliance efficiently.

What Triggers Section 106 Review?

Section 106 review is triggered whenever a federal agency carries out, approves, or funds an undertaking that has the potential to affect historic properties. This threshold is intentionally broad, capturing a wide range of project types across industries.

Common triggers include federal highway projects through FHWA, permits issued by the Army Corps of Engineers under Section 404 of the Clean Water Act, HUD-funded community development grants, FCC licenses for communications towers, and any project on federal land. Critically, the presence of a federal nexus — not the magnitude of the project — is what activates Section 106.

Private projects that might seem entirely non-federal can still be subject to Section 106 if they require a federal permit. A wetland fill permit from the Army Corps, for example, creates a federal nexus even if the project is entirely privately financed. Project sponsors should assess the federal nexus question early in planning to avoid late-stage compliance surprises.

What Are the Four Steps of the Section 106 Process?

The Section 106 process follows four sequential steps established in the ACHP's regulations at 36 CFR Part 800. Each step builds on the last and requires documented consultation with SHPOs and other parties.

Step 1 — Initiate the Process: The federal agency identifies consulting parties, including the SHPO, tribal historic preservation officers (THPOs), local governments, and interested members of the public. The agency also determines the area of potential effects (APE), the geographic area within which the undertaking may directly or indirectly affect historic properties.

Step 2 — Identify Historic Properties: The agency and its consultants conduct background research and field surveys to locate and evaluate properties within the APE. Properties are evaluated against the National Register of Historic Places (NRHP) criteria. This step often involves archaeological surveys, architectural history surveys, and consultation with tribes about traditional cultural properties.

Step 3 — Assess Adverse Effects: If historic properties are present, the agency applies the Criteria of Adverse Effect to determine whether the undertaking will harm the characteristics that make those properties significant. Effects can be direct, indirect, or cumulative.

Step 4 — Resolve Adverse Effects: When an adverse effect is found, the agency, SHPO, ACHP, and consulting parties negotiate a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) or Programmatic Agreement (PA) that outlines measures to avoid, minimize, or mitigate the harm. This might include archaeological data recovery, historic structure documentation, or design modifications.

How Is the Area of Potential Effects Defined?

The area of potential effects (APE) is the geographic area within which an undertaking may directly or indirectly cause alterations in the character or use of historic properties. Defining the APE correctly is one of the most consequential early decisions in the Section 106 process.

The APE is defined by the federal agency in consultation with the SHPO and must account for both direct and indirect effects. A road widening project, for example, may have a direct APE limited to the construction footprint but an indirect APE that extends further to capture noise and visual impacts on nearby historic districts.

Underestimating the APE is a common compliance error that can result in re-consultation, additional surveys, and project delays. GIS and remote sensing analysis is increasingly used to define APEs rigorously and to document the geographic boundaries in a defensible, reproducible way. An experienced CRM firm can help agencies and project sponsors establish an appropriate APE at the outset.

What Happens When an Adverse Effect Is Found?

A finding of adverse effect does not automatically stop a project. Rather, it initiates a structured consultation process aimed at resolving the effect through avoidance, minimization, or mitigation measures documented in a Memorandum of Agreement.

The most common mitigation measures include archaeological data recovery excavations, Historic American Buildings Survey (HABS) or Historic American Engineering Record (HAER) documentation, interpretive programs, and design modifications to reduce visual or physical impacts. The MOA is a binding agreement signed by the federal agency, the SHPO, and often the ACHP that commits all parties to the agreed-upon measures.

When adverse effects cannot be resolved through negotiation, the ACHP may issue formal comments to the head of the federal agency. Although those comments are advisory rather than mandatory, they carry significant weight and can influence agency decision-making. Reaching agreement through the MOA process is almost always preferable to an unresolved adverse effect finding.

How Does Section 106 Relate to NEPA and Other Federal Laws?

Section 106 of the NHPA is distinct from the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA), but the two processes are closely related and are frequently coordinated. Both require federal agencies to consider impacts before taking action, and both involve public and stakeholder consultation.

The ACHP's regulations at 36 CFR Part 800.8 explicitly encourage agencies to coordinate Section 106 with NEPA to avoid duplication of effort. Environmental impact statements (EIS) and environmental assessments (EA) often incorporate Section 106 findings, and the two processes share many of the same consulting parties and public involvement milestones.

Section 106 also intersects with Section 110 of the NHPA, which imposes affirmative duties on federal agencies to identify and protect historic properties in their custody, and with Section 4(f) of the Department of Transportation Act, which prohibits the use of publicly owned parks, recreation areas, wildlife refuges, and historic sites in transportation projects unless there is no feasible and prudent alternative. Understanding how these statutes interact is essential for complex, multi-agency undertakings.

Frequently Asked Questions

Who is responsible for Section 106 compliance?

The federal agency with jurisdiction over the undertaking is ultimately responsible for Section 106 compliance. However, project sponsors — developers, state agencies, or utilities — often manage the day-to-day consultation process on the agency's behalf, typically working with a qualified CRM firm to conduct surveys and prepare required documentation.

How long does the Section 106 process take?

Timeline varies significantly by project complexity. Simple undertakings with no historic properties identified can be completed in a few weeks. Projects requiring extensive archaeological surveys, adverse effect negotiations, and MOA execution may take six to eighteen months or longer. Early integration of Section 106 into project planning minimizes delays.

What is a Programmatic Agreement under Section 106?

A Programmatic Agreement (PA) is a substitute for a standard MOA used when effects cannot be fully determined in advance, when a project is unusually large or complex, or when an agency wants to establish a framework for managing Section 106 compliance across multiple future undertakings. PAs provide flexibility while ensuring ongoing consultation and mitigation.

Does Section 106 apply to archaeology only?

No. Section 106 applies to all types of historic properties eligible for or listed on the National Register of Historic Places, including historic buildings, structures, districts, landscapes, objects, and archaeological sites. It also covers traditional cultural properties of significance to Native American tribes and Native Hawaiian organizations.

What happens if Section 106 is not followed?

Failure to comply with Section 106 can result in project injunctions, requirements to redo surveys or consultation, legal challenges from consulting parties, and reputational harm. In some cases, federal agencies have been required to halt construction and conduct after-the-fact mitigation at significantly greater cost than proactive compliance.

Navigating Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act requires experienced cultural resource professionals who understand both the regulatory framework and the practical realities of field archaeology and historic preservation consultation. Tennessee Valley Archaeological Research (TVAR) provides full-service Section 106 compliance support — from APE definition and archaeological survey through SHPO consultation and MOA negotiation — for federal agencies, state DOTs, and private developers across the Southeast. Contact TVAR to discuss your project's compliance needs.
excerpt: Learn what Section 106 of the NHPA requires, how the four-step review process works, and what triggers compliance for federal projects. A complete guide.
